title: Sharp positional chest pain after a viral illness

chart.oneLiner: 30M presents with 2 days of sharp central chest pain that is worse lying flat and better leaning forward, one week after a viral upper respiratory illness.

opening: I've got this sharp pain in the middle of my chest. It's worse when I lie down and eases up when I sit forward.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 30-year-old man, uncomfortable but not in severe distress, sitting leaning forward. Cooperative.

HPI:
- Sharp, central/retrosternal chest pain for 2 days.
- POSITIONAL: worse lying supine, better sitting up and leaning forward. PLEURITIC: worse with deep breath.
- Sometimes radiates to the left trapezius ridge (only if asked).
- Had a viral upper respiratory illness (cough, runny nose, low-grade fevers) about a week ago that has mostly resolved (only if asked about recent illness).
- NO exertional trigger, NO pressure/heaviness, NO diaphoresis, NO tearing quality, NO leg swelling, NO recent travel.

OLDCARTS: onset 2 days ago; central chest; constant with pleuritic/positional exacerbation; sharp; worse supine and with inspiration, better leaning forward; severity 5/10.

PMH: none. MEDICATIONS: none. ALLERGIES: none. FAMILY: noncontributory. SOCIAL: nonsmoker, social alcohol, graduate student.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY the system/maneuver requested; give normals when asked):
- General: alert, comfortable when leaning forward.
- Vitals: BP 124/78, HR 98, RR 16, 37.6°C, SpO2 99% RA.
- Cardiac: a three-component PERICARDIAL FRICTION RUB is audible at the left lower sternal border, best with the patient leaning forward; normal S1/S2, no murmur.
- Lungs: clear bilaterally.
- Chest wall: NO reproducible tenderness.
- Neck: JVP normal, no pulsus paradoxus appreciated.
- Extremities: no edema, no calf tenderness. Abdomen: soft, nontender.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis.
- Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the recent viral illness or the trapezius radiation unless asked about recent illness or where the pain travels.
- The friction rub is only revealed if the student auscultates the heart (ideally with the patient leaning forward).
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.Troponin: Mildly elevated at 0.10 ng/mL (suggesting some myocardial involvement — myopericarditis).
labs.echo: Trivial pericardial effusion, normal biventricular function, no tamponade physiology.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential diagnosis (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Acute pericarditis (leading, 9; accepts pericarditis/myopericarditis). Acute coronary syndrome (CANT-MISS,5). Pulmonary embolism (CANT-MISS,3). Pneumonia/pleuritis (core,2). Musculoskeletal (core,2). GERD (bonus,1). Names pericarditis with supporting features — positional pleuritic pain, friction rub, recent viral illness (core,4).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Acute pericarditis. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Acute coronary syndrome; Pulmonary embolism. Also reasonable: Pneumonia/pleuritis; Musculoskeletal.

coreActions.item: Names pericarditis with supporting features — positional pleuritic pain, friction rub, recent viral illness

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: EKG (CANT-MISS,4). Troponin to assess myocardial involvement (core,3). CXR (core,2). Inflammatory markers ESR/CRP (core,2). CBC (core,2). Echocardiogram to assess for effusion/tamponade (bonus,1). PENALTY: no EKG (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; CBC; BMP; Troponin; Inflammatory markers; Echocardiogram.

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = diffuse concave-upward ST elevation with PR-segment depression; reciprocal change in aVR. CREDIT: rate & rhythm (3); identifies WIDESPREAD (non-territorial) ST elevation with PR depression (6); distinguishes from STEMI - diffuse rather than a single coronary territory (4); systematic read (2). PENALTY: misreading as an acute STEMI and activating the cath lab (-3).
keyFindings: distinguishes from STEMI - diffuse rather than a single coronary territory
criticalFindings: Diffuse concave-upward ST-segment elevation across multiple leads with PR-segment depression; reciprocal changes in aVR.
commonMistakes: misreading as an acute STEMI and activating the cath lab
steps.idealAnswer: Diffuse concave-upward ST-segment elevation across multiple leads with PR-segment depression; reciprocal changes in aVR.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Diffuse concave-upward ST-segment elevation across multiple leads with PR-segment depression; reciprocal changes in aVR.

coreActions.item: distinguishes from STEMI - diffuse rather than a single coronary territory

penalties.item: misreading as an acute STEMI and activating the cath lab

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = cardiogenic pulmonary edema: bilateral perihilar 'bat-wing' alveolar opacities with vascular redistribution. CREDIT: identifies the bilateral perihilar alveolar opacities (6); recognizes cephalization / signs of pulmonary edema (4). PENALTY: calling the study normal / missing the abnormality (-3).
criticalFindings: Pulmonary edema (bilateral alveolar opacities, vascular redistribution).
steps.idealAnswer: Cardiogenic pulmonary edema: bilateral perihilar bat-wing opacities with vascular redistribution.

criticalActions: Pulmonary edema (bilateral alveolar opacities, vascular redistribution).

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Given the diffuse ST elevation with PR depression and mild troponin rise, what is your diagnosis and how do you distinguish it from STEMI?
steps.rubric: Acute pericarditis (with myocardial involvement / myopericarditis given mild troponin) (8). Distinguishes from STEMI: DIFFUSE (not territorial) ST elevation, PR depression, no reciprocal changes except aVR, friction rub (5). Notes the small effusion without tamponade (2).

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH pericardial disease: NSAID (high-dose ibuprofen/ASA) (CANT-MISS,6); colchicine added to NSAID, reduces recurrence (CANT-MISS,6); PPI gastroprotection (2); activity restriction (2); echocardiogram to assess for effusion/tamponade (3); treat underlying cause (2). PENALTY: corticosteroids first-line (increase recurrence) (-3); anticoagulation with a pericardial effusion (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: High-dose NSAID (ibuprofen/aspirin) PLUS colchicine as first-line therapy, with a PPI for gastric protection. Activity restriction. Treat the underlying cause; avoid anticoagulation. Echocardiography to exclude effusion/tamponade. Reserve corticosteroids for refractory or specific etiologies.
steps.disposition: Usually outpatient; admit if high-risk features

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Diffuse concave-upward ST-segment elevation across multiple leads with PR-segment depression; reciprocal changes in aVR.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Bilateral perihilar 'bat-wing' alveolar opacities, vascular redistribution, peribronchial cuffing.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Bilateral perihilar 'bat-wing' alveolar opacities, vascular redistribution, peribronchial cuffing. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph showing pulmonary edema bat wing

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 30M presents with 2 days of sharp central chest pain that is worse lying flat and better leaning forward, one week after a viral upper respiratory illness. The diagnosis is Acute pericarditis, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Cardiac: a three-component PERICARDIAL FRICTION RUB is audible at the left lower sternal border, best with the patient leaning forward; normal S1/S2, no murmur.
Neck: JVP normal, no pulsus paradoxus appreciated.
Extremities: no edema, no calf tenderness. Abdomen: soft, nontender.
Extremities: no edema, no calf tenderness. Abdomen: soft, nontender.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Acute pericarditis while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Acute pericarditis and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.

Pulmonary edema (bilateral alveolar opacities, vascular redistribution).

imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
